title: Gauging the Spacetime Code
abstract: In recent years, the spacetime code has arisen as a candidate for a unifying view of fault tolerance in space and time. On the other hand, the recent study of dynamical phases has increasingly turned its attention to fault tolerance as a notion of a dynamically stable process. In this work, I explore one pathway between the two, achieved by gauging the spacetime code. This gives rise to a lattice gauge theory that inherits the elements of fault tolerance associated with a circuit, with Gauss laws corresponding to equivalence relations between configurations of spacetime errors and Wilson loops corresponding to detectors. The obtained gauge theory finds a surprisingly wide array of applications, from quantum error correction to condensed matter physics, and even learning theory: (1) It contains in its description foliated computation, and hence gives rise to one version of a gauge theory for measurement-based quantum computation. (2) For a class of topologically ordered mixed states, it gives us a gauge-theoretic language to describe the classical memory associated with the state. (3) The gauge-invariant observables of the theory which describe detectors also coincide with the learnable degrees of freedom of circuit Pauli noise.
summary: This theoretical work develops a novel gauge-theoretic description by 'gauging' the spacetime code applied to quantum circuits. It successfully unifies fault tolerance analysis with topological physics by showing that circuit redundancies map directly onto measurable detectors within the resulting lattice gauge theory. This framework has broad implications for understanding measurement-based computation and noise in quantum systems.
main_problem: The work aims to bridge the concepts of fault tolerance in quantum computation with the physics of dynamical phases of matter by constructing a gauge theory from the spacetime code.
main_result: A resulting lattice gauge theory is established that naturally incorporates fault tolerance elements, providing a unified language for describing measurement-based quantum computation and classical memory associated with topological states.
method: The authors employ techniques from lattice gauge theory by 'gauging' the spacetime code. This involves defining elementary circuit operators (ECOs) to construct an Instantaneous Stabilizer Group (ISG) that characterizes the system's evolution under noise and measurement.
model_or_system: The model centers on quantum circuits restricted to Clifford operations, which are discretized in time-spacetime. The framework uses concepts like spacetime errors, detectors (gauge invariant observables), and elementary circuit operators ($\mathcal{G}_C$).
key_observables: Gauge invariant observables (detectors), the Bare Effect $(o_f, p_f)$, and the structure of the Instantaneous Stabilizer Group $	ext{ISG}(t)$.
important_parameters: Circuit depth ($T$), number of qubits ($N$), and the specific type/location of Pauli errors (data vs. measurement).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that fault tolerance can be captured by a gauge theory structure, and it relies on defining equivalence relations between spacetime faults using elementary circuit operators.
figures_summary: Figures schematically illustrate conventions for Clifford operations across time-spacetime layers, showing the relationship between state evolution ($\psi_t$) and information geometry (ISG($t$)).
paper_structure: The paper builds logically by first defining the gauged spacetime code, then formalizing elementary circuit operators and the ISG structure. It proceeds to define fault equivalence via the 'Bare Effect' and finally proves that detectors correspond exactly to redundancies within this gauge framework.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful mathematical formalism (gauge theory) to analyze robustness in quantum computation, connecting abstract concepts of symmetry breaking/restoration to physical error correction mechanisms relevant for AMO platforms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic illustrating the conventions used in this work. In all figures, time goes from right to left. (a) A Clifford operation C comprises a Clifford unitary U and a set of measurements M. (b) We discretize spacetime locations in a Clifford circuit by grouping its elements up into Clifford operations, and placing these at half-integer time-steps. We insert spacetime locations, depicted by circles, at integer time-steps. (c) An example of a Clifford unitary: We allow the unitaries at each time-step to be arbitrarily complicated – in this case the unitary comprises two cNOT gates. We note that this circuit could also be differently discretized by separating the two cNOTs into...

Figure 2: Schematic depicting our conventions for where we evaluate states and their ISGs. The state |ψt⟩ describes the state at just before the t-th layer of spacetime locations, and ISG(t) describes the ISG of |ψt⟩. The final output state of the circuit is labelled |ψf⟩.

Figure 3: Schematic illustrating examples of elementary circuit operators associated with circuit elements. For integer time-steps, we will use blue for X-type operators and green for Z-type operators. Although the operators on half-integer time-steps are formally written as Paulis, they are in principle associated with purely classical variables. To distinguish them, we use gray. (a) Propagators associated with the example depicted in Fig. 1c. Since the Clifford operation is purely unitary, there are no measurement slices or half- integer spacetime locations. (b) Propagators and measurement slices associated with the example depicted in Fig. 1d. We omit the measurement dephasers, as they...

Figure 4: Schematic illustrating the graphical representation of the elementary circuit operators depicted in Fig. 3. Spacetime locations are associated with circular vertices, and elementary circuit operators are associated with square vertices. Each spacetime location in an integer-valued time-slice is associated with two vertices, one blue and one green, associated with X and Z operators respectively. Each spacetime location in a half-integer time-slice is associated with a single gray vertex. We connect the square vertices to spacetime locations based on their support. We do not distinguish types of square vertices. Each row in the above should be considered part of the same graph....

Figure 5: A simple example of a detector formed by a ZZ measurement followed by a cNOT gate followed by a single qubit Z measurement, and several faults which violate the detector. (a) An X error between the two measurements. (b) A readout error in the first measurement. (c) A pair of X errors just before and just after the first measurement. All three examples trigger the detector. However, while the errors in (b) and (c) are equivalent with each other, they are not equivalent to the error in (a).
